explanation.title: What is JavaScript and Where Does It Run?
explanation.content:
# What is JavaScript?

Imagine a web page as a building. HTML is the bricks and walls that give the building its structure. CSS is the paint and decoration that makes it look attractive. **JavaScript** is the electricity — it makes everything move, respond, and come alive.

JavaScript is a **programming language** created by Brendan Eich in 1995. It was originally built to make web pages interactive: validate a form before it is submitted, show a menu when a button is clicked, update a counter without reloading the page. Today it does far more.

## Where JavaScript Runs

| Environment | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Browser** | Every modern browser has a built-in JavaScript engine (Chrome uses V8, Firefox uses SpiderMonkey). Code runs when a page loads. |
| **Server (Node.js)** | JavaScript can also run on a server, outside any browser, using the Node.js runtime. |
| **Mobile** | Frameworks like React Native let you build iOS and Android apps with JavaScript. |
| **Desktop** | Electron lets you build desktop apps (VS Code itself is built with it). |

## JavaScript vs Python vs Java

| Feature | JavaScript | Python | Java |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary use | Web and server apps | Data science, scripting | Enterprise software |
| Typing | Dynamic | Dynamic | Static |
| Runs in browser | Yes — natively | No | No |
| Beginner-friendly | Yes | Very easy | Moderate |

## The Console: Your Sandbox

Every browser has a **developer console** where you can run JavaScript instantly. Open it with `F12` → Console tab. Type any JavaScript and press Enter to run it immediately.

In this course, all code examples run in the **Node.js** environment (via Judge0), which behaves like the browser console but without the DOM. The `console.log()` function is your main output tool — it works identically in both environments.

In the code example below, you will see `console.log()` used in several ways to display text, numbers, and expressions.

codeExample.title: JavaScript Says Hello
codeExample.explanation:
- `// comment` — a single-line comment; JavaScript ignores everything after `//` on that line
- `/* ... */` — a multi-line comment; everything between the delimiters is ignored
- `console.log("Hello, World!")` — calls the built-in `console.log()` function; the argument is displayed in the console
- `console.log("Years since:", 2025 - 1995)` — multiple arguments are separated by a space; arithmetic is evaluated first
- The semicolon `;` terminates a statement; JavaScript can often infer it, but writing it explicitly avoids subtle bugs

exercise.title: Write Your JavaScript Introduction
exercise.instructions:
Write a JavaScript program that logs four lines to the console: your name on line 1, your city on line 2, your age as a number on line 3, and your favourite programming language on line 4. Use console.log() for each line. A number does not need quotes; text must be in quotes.

Expected output example:
Priya Sharma
Mumbai
21
JavaScript
hints: 💡 Each piece of information needs its own console.log() call on a separate line.
💡 Text values like your name and city must be inside quotes; a number like your age must not have quotes.
💡 Copy the pattern of the first two console.log() calls and add two more below them for age and language.

questions.question: Which function is used to display output in the JavaScript console?
questions.explanation: console.log() is JavaScript's primary output function. It writes its arguments to the browser console or terminal (in Node.js). Python uses print(), but JavaScript uses console.log(). There is no print() or display() function in standard JavaScript.

questions.question: What does the // symbol do at the start of a line in JavaScript?
options: Divides the value on the left by the value on the right
Starts a single-line comment that JavaScript ignores
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, // starts a single-line comment. Everything from // to the end of that line is ignored by the JavaScript engine when the program runs. Comments are notes written by the programmer for other humans. For multi-line comments, use /* ... */.

questions.question: Where can JavaScript code run natively, without any extra installation?
options: Only on a dedicated JavaScript server
questions.explanation: Every modern browser — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge — includes a built-in JavaScript engine. This means any JavaScript code can run directly in a browser without installing anything. This is what makes JavaScript the only language that runs natively on both the client (browser) and server (Node.js).

explanation.title: Storing Values with let, const, and var
explanation.content:
# Variables in JavaScript

A **variable** is a named container for storing a value. Think of it as a labelled jar: you put something inside, give the jar a name, and later you pick it up by that name. JavaScript has three keywords for creating variables: `let`, `const`, and `var`.

## let — the modern default for changeable values

```javascript
let score = 0;
score = 10;       // reassignment is allowed
score = score + 5; // score is now 15
```

Use `let` when the value will change over time.

## const — for values that should not change

```javascript
const PI = 3.14159;
const MAX_USERS = 100;
// PI = 3;   // TypeError — cannot reassign a const
```

Use `const` when the value must stay fixed. The name does not need to be uppercase, but uppercase is the convention for true constants.

## var — the legacy keyword (avoid in modern code)

`var` was the only option before 2015. It still works but has confusing scoping rules that `let` and `const` fixed. You will encounter it in older codebases, but prefer `let`/`const` for new code.

## Naming Rules

| Rule | Valid | Invalid | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start with letter, `$`, or `_` | `score` | `1score` | Cannot start with digit |
| Letters, digits, `$`, `_` only | `user_name` | `user-name` | Hyphens not allowed |
| Case-sensitive | `Score` ≠ `score` | | |
| Not a keyword | `total` | `let` | Reserved word |

By convention, JavaScript uses **camelCase** for variable names: `firstName`, `totalScore`, `isLoggedIn`.

## Declaring Without Initialising

```javascript
let city;             // declared but not yet assigned
console.log(city);    // undefined
city = "Delhi";       // now it has a value
```

A declared but unassigned variable holds the special value `undefined`.

In the code example below, you will see `let` and `const` used together in a realistic ticket booking scenario.

codeExample.title: Cinema Ticket Booking
codeExample.explanation:
- `const TICKET_PRICE = 200` — a constant; attempting to reassign it would throw a `TypeError` at runtime
- `let seatsBooked = 0` — a mutable variable; its value will change as tickets are sold
- `seatsBooked += 3` — shorthand for `seatsBooked = seatsBooked + 3`; updates the variable in place
- `MAX_SEATS - seatsBooked` — arithmetic on a constant and a variable; constants can be read but not reassigned
- `customerName = "Rahul"` — reassigning a `let` variable is legal; the old value `"Ananya"` is replaced

exercise.title: Build a Shopping Cart Tracker
exercise.instructions:
Create a shopping cart program. Declare const ITEM_PRICE = 350, let itemCount = 0, and let cartTotal = 0. Set itemCount to 4 and compute cartTotal as itemCount * ITEM_PRICE. Apply a 10% discount: compute discountAmount = cartTotal * 0.10 and finalTotal = cartTotal - discountAmount. Print itemCount, cartTotal, discountAmount, and finalTotal.

Expected output:
Items: 4
Cart Total: 1400
Discount: 140
Final Total: 1260
hints: 💡 Add two more console.log() calls — one for discountAmount and one for finalTotal.
💡 Follow the same pattern: console.log("Discount:", discountAmount); and console.log("Final Total:", finalTotal);
💡 The discount is already calculated — you only need to print it with the correct label.

questions.question: Which keyword should you use when a variable's value will never change?
questions.explanation: const declares a constant — a variable whose binding cannot be reassigned after creation. Attempting to reassign a const throws a TypeError. Use const by default and switch to let only when you know the value must change. var is the legacy keyword from before 2015 and should be avoided in modern JavaScript.

questions.question: What is the value of a let variable that has been declared but not yet assigned?
questions.explanation: A declared but unassigned variable holds the special value undefined. This is JavaScript's way of saying 'this variable exists but has no value yet'. It is different from null, which is an explicit 'no value' that a programmer assigns intentionally, and from 0 or "" which are valid values of specific types.

explanation.title: JavaScript's Seven Primitive Data Types
explanation.content:
# Data Types in JavaScript

Every value in a program has a **type** — a category that determines what kind of operations you can perform on it. You cannot multiply a name by a city, but you can multiply a price by a quantity. JavaScript has seven **primitive** types.

## The Seven Primitive Types

| Type | Example values | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| `string` | `"hello"`, `'Rahul'` | Text |
| `number` | `42`, `3.14`, `-7` | All numbers (integers and decimals) |
| `boolean` | `true`, `false` | Yes/no, on/off decisions |
| `undefined` | `undefined` | Declared but not yet assigned |
| `null` | `null` | Explicitly empty — no value |
| `bigint` | `9007199254740991n` | Integers too large for number |
| `symbol` | `Symbol("id")` | Unique identifiers (advanced) |

For beginner code, you will use `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `undefined`, and `null` most of the time.

## Strings in Detail

Strings can use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks (template literals):

```javascript
const a = 'single quotes';
const b = "double quotes";    // same result
const c = `template literal`; // backticks allow embedded expressions

const name  = "Priya";
const score = 95;
const msg   = `${name} scored ${score} marks.`; // interpolation
```

Template literals (backticks) are the modern preferred style because they support **string interpolation** — embedding expressions directly inside the string with `${}`.

## The typeof Operator

`typeof` returns a string describing the type of a value:

```javascript
typeof 42           // "number"
typeof "hello"      // "string"
typeof true         // "boolean"
typeof undefined    // "undefined"
typeof null         // "object"  ← famous JavaScript quirk!
```

The `typeof null` returning `"object"` is a known historical bug in JavaScript that was never fixed for backwards-compatibility reasons.

## Type Coercion Warning

JavaScript automatically converts types in some situations — this can produce surprising results:

```javascript
"5" + 3     // "53"  — number 3 coerced to string
"5" - 3     // 2     — string "5" coerced to number
```

The `+` operator with a string triggers **concatenation**, not addition. Always be explicit about types when doing arithmetic. In the code example below, you will see all five common types used and inspected with `typeof`.

codeExample.title: Exploring JavaScript Types
codeExample.explanation:
- `const templateDemo = \`Product: ${productName}\`` — a template literal; `${}` embeds any expression inside the string
- `price * (1 - taxRate)` — standard arithmetic; parentheses control evaluation order
- `let promoCode;` — declared without assignment; its value is `undefined` until explicitly set
- `const selectedItem = null` — explicitly set to `null`; signals deliberate absence of a value
- `typeof null` — returns `"object"`, a well-known historical JavaScript quirk, not a bug in your code
- `.toFixed(2)` — a number method that formats the value to exactly two decimal places as a string

exercise.title: Type Inspector Program
exercise.instructions:
Declare five variables: userName (a string), userAge (a number), isPremium (a boolean), referralCode (set to null), and lastLogin (declared but not assigned, so it is undefined). For each variable, use console.log() to print the variable's value and its type using typeof, separated by a pipe character. Use template literals for the output.

Expected output format:
rahul | string
25 | number
true | boolean
null | object
undefined | undefined
hints: 💡 Follow the exact same pattern as the first two lines: console.log(`${variable} | ${typeof variable}`);
💡 typeof null returns 'object' — this is expected JavaScript behaviour, not an error.
💡 typeof lastLogin returns 'undefined' because the variable was declared but never assigned a value.

questions.question: What does the typeof operator return for the value null?
questions.explanation: typeof null returns "object" — this is a well-known historical bug in JavaScript that was never corrected because fixing it would break millions of existing programs. To correctly check for null, use strict equality: value === null. Do not rely on typeof for detecting null.

questions.question: What is the result of "5" + 3 in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: When the + operator encounters a string on either side, JavaScript converts the other operand to a string and concatenates rather than adds. So "5" + 3 produces the string "53", not the number 8. Use Number("5") + 3 or parseInt("5") + 3 to force numeric addition.

questions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined in JavaScript?
options: They are identical and can always be used interchangeably
null means a variable was declared but not assigned; undefined means it was explicitly set to empty
undefined means declared but not assigned; null is an explicit intentional empty value
null is for numbers; undefined is for strings
questions.explanation: undefined is the automatic value JavaScript assigns when a variable is declared but not given a value. null is a value that a programmer assigns explicitly to signal 'intentionally no value'. Think of undefined as 'not yet known' and null as 'known to be empty'.
